Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I felt like it or when deadlines were breathing down my neck. I learn better by doing things hands-on anyway, so sitting down with a schedule never really worked for me. I'd often end up studying at weird hours, like late at night when the house was quiet.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, to be honest. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram everything into a couple of days. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd actually sit down and focus. I also had extracurriculars that took up chunks of my time, so I'd work around those. Regular study times just weren't my thing—I'd study when I felt the pressure building.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check if something was due soon or to submit an assignment. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that would remind me to log in. I wasn't one of those people who checked it daily just to stay on top of things. Fear of missing a deadline was probably my biggest motivator, if I'm being real. That anxious feeling in my chest would push me to finally open it up.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me—I always had to consciously remind myself to check it. There were maybe a few weeks where I got into a rhythm, but then something would throw me off and I'd fall out of it. I'd say I used the LMS more reactively than proactively. It was more like a tool I grabbed when I needed it rather than something woven into my daily life.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty helpful because I could watch it at my own pace and rewind parts I didn't get. I also liked when there were practical examples or interactive stuff—that fits how I learn best. Discussion posts were okay because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me. The assignment submission portal was straightforward, which I appreciated since I didn't want to deal with complicated processes.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the extra reading materials and supplementary resources. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably didn't touch it. I also didn't engage much with the forums unless I had to for marks. It felt like there was so much content that I just focused on what seemed essential. Some of the longer videos I'd skim through rather than watch properly.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to my grades or an upcoming assignment, I'd actually put effort into it. Activities that were hands-on or had clear, practical applications felt worthwhile to me. If something seemed like busywork or I couldn't see the point, my motivation just tanked. I also valued stuff that didn't take forever—I wanted efficiency, not endless reading.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like it was negotiable, so I'd usually skip it unless I was really interested or had extra time, which was rare. Required activities had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade. With optional things, there was no consequence for skipping, so my anxious brain would prioritize the stuff that could hurt me if I didn't do it. It's not that I didn't care—I just had to triage.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs and study from those without being logged in. I also used YouTube videos and other websites to understand concepts better because sometimes the course materials didn't click for me. I had notebooks where I'd write things out by hand—that helps me remember stuff better than just reading on a screen. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper, and none of that would show up anywhere online. I'd also study with friends sometimes, talking through concepts or quizzing each other. If I was reviewing downloaded materials on my tablet during my commute, the LMS wouldn't know. My best studying often happened when I was physically doing something, not clicking around online.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or take notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. It was just more convenient to have stuff saved locally so I could access it whenever without needing internet or logging in. I'd review those materials multiple times offline, but the system would only show that one initial access.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel scared about falling behind, which ironically made it harder to actually engage. Sometimes I'd avoid the LMS because opening it stressed me out—like I didn't want to see how much I had to do. My extracurriculars also got intense at certain points, pulling my attention away from coursework.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd just do the bare minimum on the LMS. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just close the tab instead of pushing through. My other responsibilities, like activities and social stuff, definitely competed for my time. On high-stress days, I'd convince myself I'd catch up later and then barely log in.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I was basically zoned out. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch a video but be thinking about other stuff the whole time. It looked like engagement on paper, but mentally I wasn't really there. That happened more when my anxiety was high.
Q15: I avoided things that felt overwhelming or that I didn't understand well. If an assignment seemed too complex, I'd push it off until the last possible moment. It's like my brain would rather deal with the panic of a deadline than the discomfort of struggling with hard material early. I knew it was self-sabotage, but in the moment, avoidance felt easier. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me recognize this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done much at all. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become super active on the LMS, downloading stuff, watching videos, submitting work. My usage was very spiky because of this—quiet periods followed by intense bursts. It's not a healthy pattern, but it's how I operated this semester.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me. Positive feedback gave me a little boost and made me want to engage more. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd avoid that course for a bit. If feedback came quickly, I was more likely to apply it to the next thing. But if it took forever, I'd already moved on mentally and it didn't impact much.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really force me to stay consistent. If there had been more frequent small deadlines, I probably would've engaged more steadily. The flexibility was nice in theory, but for someone like me who drifts, it meant I could procrastinate easily. I needed more external structure to keep me on track, honestly.
Q19: Having clear due dates and organized modules made things easier when I actually looked at them. But the sheer amount of content was overwhelming—it made me want to disengage rather than dive in. I would've appreciated more interactive or hands-on elements since that's how I learn best. The discussion requirements felt a bit forced, which made me less enthusiastic about participating genuinely.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or scared about the course, so they keep checking and clicking around but can't bring themselves to actually complete and submit work. It's like being stuck in preparation mode without executing. Or maybe they're confused and looking for answers but not finding what they need. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in super often, but I still managed to do okay because I was studying offline a lot. I'd download materials and work through them on my own time. My assignment completion was high even though my attendance and login frequency weren't impressive. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything that goes into learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and when you submitted stuff. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you were actually focused or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline studying, discussions with classmates, or the mental effort you put in. They also can't show your emotional state, like whether anxiety was blocking you from engaging meaningfully.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and wanting to improve. Engagement is when the material sticks with you and you can apply it. It's more internal than any system can measure.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my engagement patterns, or how scared I felt about falling behind. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my conversations with friends about course content, or the times I was mentally checked out while technically logged in. It also wouldn't show the effort I put into managing my mental health through CBT just to be able to function in the course. There's a whole emotional layer that data just can't see.
